content:
# John Chapter 21 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/JHN_021_page_001.jpg

1. After this Jesus revealed himself again to the disciples by the Sea of Tiberias, and he revealed himself in this way.

2. Simon Peter, Thomas (called the Twin), Nathanael of Cana in Galilee, the sons of Zebedee, and two others of his disciples were together.

3. Simon Peter said to them, "I am going fishing." They said to him, "We will go with you." They went out and got into the boat, but that night they caught nothing.

4. Just as day was breaking, Jesus stood on the shore; yet the disciples did not know that it was Jesus.

5. Jesus said to them, "Children, do you have any fish?" They answered him, "No."

6. He said to them, "Cast the net on the right side of the boat, and you will find some." So they cast it, and now they were not able to haul it in, because of the quantity of fish.

7. That disciple whom Jesus loved therefore said to Peter, "It is the Lord!" When Simon Peter heard that it was the Lord, he put on his outer garment, for he was stripped for work, and threw himself into the sea.

8. The other disciples came in the boat, dragging the net full of fish, for they were not far from the land, but about a hundred yards off.

9. When they got out on land, they saw a charcoal fire in place, with fish laid out on it, and bread.

10. Jesus said to them, "Bring some of the fish that you have just caught."

11. So Simon Peter went aboard and hauled the net ashore, full of large fish, 153 of them. And although there were so many, the net was not torn.

12. Jesus said to them, "Come and have breakfast." Now none of the disciples dared ask him, "Who are you?" They knew it was the Lord.

13. Jesus came and took the bread and gave it to them, and so with the fish.

14. This was now the third time that Jesus was revealed to the disciples after he was raised from the dead.

15. When they had finished breakfast, Jesus said to Simon Peter, "Simon, son of John, do you love me more than these?" He said to him, "Yes, Lord; you know that I love you." He said to him, "Feed my lambs."

16. He said to him a second time, "Simon, son of John, do you love me?" He said to him, "Yes, Lord; you know that I love you." He said to him, "Tend my sheep."

17. He said to him the third time, "Simon, son of John, do you love me?" Peter was grieved because he said to him the third time, "Do you love me?" and he said to him, "Lord, you know everything; you know that I love you." Jesus said to him, "Feed my sheep.

18. Truly, truly, I say to you, when you were young, you used to dress yourself and walk wherever you wanted, but when you are old, you will stretch out your hands, and another will dress you and carry you where you do not want to go."

19. (This he said to show by what kind of death he was to glorify God.) And after saying this he said to him, "Follow me."

20. Peter turned and saw the disciple whom Jesus loved following them, the one who also had leaned back against him during the supper and had said, "Lord, who is it that is going to betray you?"

21. When Peter saw him, he said to Jesus, "Lord, what about this man?"

22. Jesus said to him, "If it is my will that he remain until I come, what is that to you? You follow me!"

23. So the saying spread abroad among the brothers that this disciple was not going to die; yet Jesus did not say to him that he was not going to die, but, "If it is my will that he remain until I come, what is that to you?"

24. This is the disciple who is bearing witness about these things, and who has written these things, and we know that his testimony is true.

25. Now there are also many other things that Jesus did. Were every one of them to be written, I suppose that the world itself could not contain the books that would be written.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

The Epilogue. Whether written by the same author as chapters 1-20 or by his community, chapter 21 addresses two pastoral concerns that the original ending left unresolved: Peter's restoration after his denial and the fate of the Beloved Disciple. It is a chapter of breakfasts, fires, and second chances.

**Verse 9 — The charcoal fire:** EMET and LOGOS both note the ἀνθρακιά connection to 18:18. PNEUMA provides the deepest reading: "Jesus recreates the scene of Peter's worst moment and replaces the memory. Healing happens in the same place as wounding." SOPHIA affirms: this is among the most psychologically and pastorally astute details in the NT. Jesus does not avoid the scene of failure — he redeems it.

**Verses 15-17 — The threefold restoration:** EMET notes the love vocabulary debate (ἀγαπάω vs. φιλέω). LOGOS traces the three denials / three questions pattern. PNEUMA captures the pastoral heart: "The only qualification for shepherding God's people is not competence... it's love." SOPHIA affirms: whether the vocabulary shift is significant or stylistic, the threefold structure is clearly deliberate — three denials require three restorations. Peter is not merely forgiven; he is recommissioned. Failure is not disqualification.

**Verse 19 — "Follow me":** LOGOS connects this to the original call in Mark 1:17. PNEUMA adds: "The call to 'follow me' now includes a cross." SOPHIA affirms: "Follow me" is both the first and last command of Peter's discipleship. The content of the command has deepened — it now includes suffering and death — but the call itself has not changed. The same Jesus who called Peter from his nets now calls him from his failure.

**Verse 22 — "What is that to you?":** PNEUMA's reading is definitive: "Mind your own calling. Don't compare." SOPHIA affirms: the last recorded command of Jesus in the Gospel is a rebuke of comparison. Each disciple has a unique path. Peter's path leads to crucifixion. The Beloved Disciple's path leads to testimony. Both are valid. Both are faithful. Neither should envy the other.

**Verse 24 — Authorship:** EMET notes the community voice ("we know"). LOGOS identifies the Beloved Disciple as the authority behind the Gospel. SOPHIA affirms: v.24 provides the Gospel's credentials — eyewitness testimony, authenticated by a community. The "we" is the Johannine community standing behind their master teacher's witness.

**Verse 25 — The final word:** The Gospel ends not with closure but with overflow. PNEUMA reads it as extravagance. SOPHIA affirms: this hyperbole is the fitting end to a Gospel that began with "all things were made through him" (1:3). The Word through whom the world was made has done more in that world than the world can record. The book ends, but the story continues.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
